title: practical english usage - Part 110
content:
To  refer to the time of repeated events, both singular and plural expressions are / sometimes throw stones ...- only one stone is thrown each time) I sometimes throw a stone into the river and wish for good luck. (clearer than Singular nouns may also be used to avoid misunderstanding.

She often goes for a ride over the hills before supper.

I often get a headache when I've been working on the computer.

When  details of the time or situation are given, nouns are often singular.

She sometimes goes for rides over the hills.

I often get headaches. (More natural than I often get a headache.) possible. When  no details are given, plural nouns are more natural.

In descriptions of repeated single events, singular and plural nouns are both 2  repeated       events not They were all anxious to increase their knowledge. (     ... their knowledges.) Uncountable nouns cannot of course be used in the plural.

Six people lost their lives in the accident.

Tell the children to blow their noses. (     ... to blow their nose.) Plural forms are almost always used in this case if there are possessives.

Tell the kids to bring a raincoat...) Tell the kids to bring raincoats to school tomorrow. (More natural than plural noun for the repeated idea.

To  talk about several people each doing the same thing, English usually prefers a 1  people     doing     the   same    thing bring      raincoats.         _____________ 131  distributive        plural:    Tell    them      to grammar «132      turning verbs into nouns: a cough, a taste For details of 'action-nouns' with have, and a list of common expressions, ►    23.

Let's have a swim. (More natural than Let's swim.) unsystematic recreational activity.

These structures are very common when we  talk about casual, unplanned or She does a bit of painting but she doesn't like to show people.

We   can use -ing forms in a similar way after do (►   435.3).

I try to go for a run every day.  Let's go for a walk.

Go for... is common with nouns for physical activity (►   475).

I don't know the answer, but I'm going to make a guess.

If it won't start, let's give it a push.

I like to have/take a bath before I go to bed.

Just take a look at yourself Let your sister have a go on the swing. (BrE) Let's have a talk about your plans.

I'U have a think and let you know what I decide, (informal BrE) take, give, make, go for.

Nouns of this kind are often introduced by 'general-purpose' verbs such as have, 2 common       structures What about a drink?   Come on - one more try!

I need a rest.       Would you like a taste?

There was a loud crash.        Did I hear a cough?

especially common in an informal style.

of this kind often have the same form as the related verbs. The  structure is It is very common to refer to an action by using a noun instead of a verb. My, your, etc (►   143)  are possessives:       terminology much for practical purposes: they rarely cause misunderstandings.

entries should help a good deal. Also, article mistakes do not usually matter very However, much article use is regular; and the explanations in the following We took a wrong turning and went to the wrong address.

I've got backache. (BrE)   I've got a headache.

expression, for no very good reason.

happen to use the in one expression, and a or no article in another similar •    Sometimes article use is more a matter of vocabulary than grammar: we when talking about things in general (►   140).

always use them in the same way as their English equivalents, especially •    Languages which  have articles (e.g. French, Swedish, Portuguese) do not •    These meanings are relatively abstract and not easy to explain.
